content.full_text:
Farn Polrrrcal PnacrrcEs CouvrrssroN
428 J Street o Suite 620 r Sacramento, CA 95g14-2329
(916) 322-5660 o Fax (916) 322-0986
November 21,2005
Dominick V. Spatafora
Legal Consultant
NORCAL
560 Davis Street, 2"d Floor
SanFrancisco, CA g4lll
Re:
Your Request for Advice
Our FiIe No. ,4,-05-208
Dear Mr. Spatafora:
This letter is in response to yourrequest for advice on behalf of NORCAL Mutual
Insurance Co. regarding campaign rules under the Political Reform Act (the ..a.i,; t----*-
QUESTION
Is it permissible under the Act for NORCAL Mutual Insurance Co. to offer its
ernployees paid time off in return for their contributions to its political action committee?
CONCLUSION
The Act does not prevent NORCAL from offering its employees paid time off in
refurn for their contributions to NORCAL's political actiin committee. However, in
transactions under the program you describe, the employees would be regarded as inter-
mediaries for the true contributor, which would be Norical,. Both the Jmployees and
the recipient committee would be required to disclose this relationship.
FACTS
NORCAL Mutual lnsurance company (.,NORCAL,,) created a califomia
political action committee last year called the MICRA california pAC (the ,.pAc,,).
Half of the PAC's activities are dedicated to education and the other haif to fundraising.
In order to generate_rll"..* and energy around your pAC you may implernent a program
that would reward NORCAL employees with paid time ofi(,?tci,,; ifthey contribute to
' Government Code sections g1000 - 91014
18109-18997, of the California Code ofRegulations.
Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections


File No. 4-05-208
Page No. 2
the PAC. Thus for example, employees who contribute $100 to the pAC might get a
half-day off, and those who contribute $150 might receive a full day of pro]
ANALYSIS
The term "contribution" is defined at section 82015 and regulation 1g215 to
include "arty payment made for political purposes.,, (Regulation t-sztsla).) .,payment,,
is defined at section 82044 to include a "transfer, loan, advance, deposit, lift or other
lendering of money, property, services or anything else of value, wletheitangible or
intangible." Thus any incentive offered by NORCAL for a contribution to thI pAC, if it
is a tangible or intangible thing of value, might also be classified as a.,contribution,, to
the PAC since it would be a payment made for a political purpose - namely, to generate
another contribution to the pAC.2
The paid service of an employee is presumptively a thing of value which NORCAL would
forego in exchange for a monetary contribution to the pAC.
' Regulation 18423, which keats payments forpersonal services as contributions and
expenditures, does not govern incentive plans whose end product is monetary contributions to a specific
recipient.
However, we do not believe that it is appropriate under these facts to classify the
PTO incentive as a contribution to the PAC. Under the incentive program you describe,
the PAC would not receive two separate contributions each time an "irptoy"" tut ".
advantage of the program. The PAC would simply receive a payment in money from a
NORCAL employee, who in turn would be reimbursed by PTO from NORCAi., a non-
monetary payment used as a medium of exchange between NORCAL and the employee.
We conclude therefore that the employee's role in the transaction is that of an inter-
mediary, who delivers a monetary contribution to the PAC in exchange for pTO from
NORCAL.3
The Act does not prohibit the kind of transaction you propose. However, the Act
does require that the true source of a contribution be properlyidentified and reported
(section 84301) and, more particularly, section S$T,provides that no contribution be
presented by an intermediary without fulI and accurate disclosure of the parties involved
in the transaction. Regulation 18432.5(b) and (c) outlines the reporting obligations of the
intermediary and the recipient, as follows:
(b) AnV person who qualifies as an intermediary for the
making of a contribution shall disclose to the reiipient of
the contribution both his or her own full name and street
address, occupation, and the name of his or her employer, if
any, or his or her principar place of business if he or she is
self-employed, and the full name and street address,
occupation, and the name of employer, if any, or principal
place ofbusiness if self-employed, of the contributor. 
-


File No. ,{-05-208
Page No. 3
(c) The recipient of the contribution shall include in his or
her campaign statement the name of the intermediary and
other information disclosed pursuant to Government code
Section 94302 and subdivision (b) of this section, if the
recipient knows or has reason to know that acontribution is
made by an intermediary.
- 
Finally, because NORCAL would be regarded as the true contributor in each of
the transactions effected under the incentive plai you describe, NoRCAL should be
mindful of the contribution limit prescribed ut .""iio, 85303(a), which proviJes ttrat:
(a) A person may not make to any committee, other than a
political party committee, md a committee other than a
political party committee may not accept, any contribution
totaling more than five thousand dollars ($5,000) per
calendar year for the purpose of making contributions to
candidates for elective state office.a
To recapifulate, nothing under the Act prohibits the incentive program you
describe, but it is necessary to rccognize that the true contributor in such transactions
would be NoRCAL, and to fully and accurately account for the payments actually
received by the PAC. section 84302 and regull ion l843t2.51quotla above) jetail the
disclosure and reporting obligations that both the involvedemployees and the recipient
PAC must satisfu' Any NoRCAL contributions to the PAC that are made for the pu{pose
of making contributions to candidates for elective state office may not exceed $5,600, in
total, duringany calendar year.
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.
Sincerely,
Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel
7.
By: Lawrence T. Woodlock
Counsel, Legal Division
LTW:rd
I:\Adviceltrs\05-208
t This amo"'t i: jdlTjed biennially for inflation. The current limit under this section is $5,600
(see regulation 185a5(a)(7).) Note too that fuoncal would incur additional reporting obligations as a
"major donor" under section g20l 3(c), if its totar contributions ,.u"t "J 
$ t 0,000 withfi u 
"ui"nau, y"u..

transactions under the program you describe, the employees would be regarded as inter-
mediaries for the true contributor, which would be Norical,. Both the Jmployees and
the recipient committee would be required to disclose this relationship.
